summary: Gruber argues that the conventional narrative about Apple's revocation of Epic's EU developer account is wrong on a key detail: Apple leadership never consciously approved Epic's new account in the first place. Epic simply enrolled through an automated online form and received approval without executive review, then announced their marketplace plans publicly. Only after that announcement did Phil Schiller and Apple's lawyers engage, ultimately revoking the account citing Epic's history of rule-breaking. While Gruber finds this distinction meaningful, he acknowledges Apple played the situation terribly and that the nuance will be lost, since to everyone outside Apple it looked like retaliation for criticism.
key_points: Epic's new developer account was approved through an automated enrollment system without executive review, not through a deliberate high-level decision by Apple leadership
Apple's revocation was framed internally as consistent with the original Fortnite ban — citing Epic's pattern of denigrating terms before breaking them — not as punishment for tweets
The distinction between automated approval and executive approval matters for understanding Apple's intent, but is irrelevant to how it played publicly
Apple's inability to foresee how revoking the account would look to developers, regulators, and the press is entirely their own failure
The EU's DMA has fundamentally changed the ground rules, and Apple's resistance to accepting that is the underlying problem
money_quotes: I'm farting into the wind by writing about this somewhat subtle distinction, because the conventional wisdom isn't going to change.
That Apple couldn't see how this would play is on them.

To almost everyone outside Apple Park — most especially third-party developers who have been, for years, souring on Apple's App Store policies — it looks not like a policy of 'We're not going to reinstate Developer Program privileges to a proven rule-breaker' but instead a retaliatory policy of 'We'll terminate the account of any developer who speaks out against us.'
The whole App Store bureaucracy is Apple's. (Or as Sweeney aptly called it tonight, 'Apple's App DMV'.)
Apple played this whole thing terribly.

It doesn't make any sense for Schiller to have asked that on February 23 if any senior Apple executives had considered the implications and explicitly approved Epic's new developer account two weeks earlier.
key_insight: Apple's real mistake wasn't malice or retaliation but institutional incompetence: their automated systems created expectations their executives then publicly shattered, and they couldn't see how terrible that would look.
